item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A strange combination of being both too long and still managing to have an ending that feels incredibly rushed. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Loved the concept, but in general I love stories based on the idea that human thoughts influence other more-or-less-physical worlds.
And I liked the usage of it on a dystopian existence where Mozart is the Big Brother. Definitely terrible.

Yet, I must say that I would have enjoyed it more if:
1) it was a faster read. Really, I liked the idea and the prose works here, while I am not a big fan SCP entries heavy on the prose aspect, but it should have been shorter for maximum effect IMO.
It's an issue being of being prose, format screw or whatever, but of simple words usage. It was a good read, mind it, but this doesn't change that a faster development could convey the same emotions and ideas while making it an easier read for some people.

2) I like having the perpsective of a member of this society, but as a reader and "possible member of the Foundation" I would have liked to read the rest, what he couldn't have seen because he was an anomalous being.
I mean, yeah, it's obvious from the start that The Foundation as no interest in changing anything about the SCP, as it is a self-contained world and is said so, but a closing note or some additional addendum about the changes through time of the SCP itself would have made the article have a better closure.

Still +1 tho.

item.title: Re: RESERVED FOR AUTHOR POST
item.content:encoded:
Source: https://www.flickr.com/photos/141378703@N03/39407701051
Author: mahsa_mz66
Licence: CC BY 2.0

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dystopian classical musician afterlife dominated by the all-seeing eye of Big Mozart and his fame-based Orwellian regime? That’s a +1 all on its lonesome.

That said, much as I love reading the doomed struggles of poor Winston Smith Johann Pachelbel, I have to concur with the general consensus that a little more showing and a little less telling would perhaps strengthen the piece. With that in mind, one wonders what this world’s version of Room 101 would look like…

Furthermore, unless I’m missing something outside of the SCP report itself the link to the Foundation seems a little tenuous. How and why do the SCP and the Foundation have such a link that enables the inhabitants of the SCP to have access to the Foundation file on it? What is the basis of their apparent deal / alliance? Some stronger connection back to the Foundation (perhaps more of a sense that they have some kind of interest or benefit going on here) would help.

Still, overall really enjoyed it. And I can’t help but wonder; if Big Mozart here can turn Musician Heaven into his own personal Airstrip One through the force of how famous he is, one can’t help but wonder what would happen if John Lennon or Elvis were to show up…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had no memory of reading this, yet I had already upvoted it.

Hmm.

Anyways, as a classically trained violinist who, for lack of a better term, detests Mozart, I adored this. The namedrops were entertaining and I loved the concept. However, I do agree with what a lot of other people had said - there's a lot of stream-of-consciousness narration going on here, and while I do think it evokes the atmosphere you're going for, it could use a few trims here and there (if you ever want to come back to this, of course. If not, I think it's fine the way it is!)

item.title: Reply
item.content:encoded: I can’t upvote a comment so I’m just gonna say UPVOTE

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I never knew I needed something like this. This is fantastically unique and hilarious. I feel like it could use a bit more plot and a less internal reflection, but I think that contributes more than it detracts.

Well-deserved +1.

(and, of course, I very much got a kick out of the rant reference that you threw in there.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1984 but it’s brozart?

I see that some people don’t like the format screw, but I think it works

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"You will be hollow. We shall squeeze you empty and then we shall fill you with ourselves."

But yeah, the tale parts are a bit overlong and don't really explore the world. It's just tells us what this one person thinks about it and his very slow realization.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A 1984 homage I didn't know I needed. +1 duly granted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like your ClassicaLoid fan fiction. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting SCP for sure, but I'm not a fan of the format screw. This is definitely one of those articles that (in my personal opinion) should either be split into an SCP and a tale, or should be reformatted to tell the same story within the confines of the traditional SCP format. While I admit that this is incredibly well-written and the overarching concepts at work are great, I can't get past the whole tale/SCP amalgam thing.

-1 for now, but I look forward to seeing how your revisions turn out and perhaps what you decide to do with the formatting (if you decide to do anything at all).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is too long! It reads like a first draft, before you have time to go in and take out repetition and unnecessary parts.

Still, I enjoyed it. The concept is both funny and horrifying. +1'd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really want to like this. The world is certainly interesting, and it's inundated in enough knowledge that it feels authentic.

That being said, I think far too much of this in conveyed through internal monologue. A good portion of the story has the viewpoint character just sittong around and thinking about the world, instead of exploring it in any meaningful manner.

It does start off strong enough for this, but once they find the computer everything comes to a complete standstill. I would consider thinking of other ways to intersperse the document portions with moments of action and showing the world instead of merely telling us about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, I love niche articles. I remember thinking well after I had submitted my entry that I wish I would have done one about the history of Western music, as it is one of my favorite topics. I don't think I couldn't have come up with something half this good.

This is visually effective and it blends the prose well, though I think it is slightly heavy on the narrative end of the scale. I find myself wanting to see the non-abridged version of the article, and I think that is a missed opportunity here, but one that is correctable.

I love the idea of Mozart being a tyrannical dictator and the subtle jibes about his music making you smarter for listening to it. Casting Pachelbel as the protagonist was clever, especially because I could have sworn it was (the fictional) Salieri (from Amadeus), as was his post-humous status as a one-hit wonder.

Thank you for writing this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmmmmm.

I really like the actual document here. Describing an alternate dimension where musicians and composers are ranked by popularity is a fantastic idea, one in which I really wish you explored more. Excerpts of initial votes. People discussing post-humorous surges in popularity. Discussion of movies made about them and the influence on their caste. You really had me wanting to know more about this world you built.

Unfortunately, the parts I liked were drowned in heavy-handed exposition dumps that feel like a sampling of the most noteworthy parts of 1984 mixed with a limited who is who of musical history. shaggy's 001 proposal and 3001 have shown that this mix of prose and skip can do well. However, here you lack the raw/horrific imagery of the former, and the emotional gut punch of the latter and we are instead left with something quite dry.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Someone got grumpy in music history class, I see. 😊
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is… this is quite a thing.

Normally, I'm prejudiced against prose in SCP articles, but I don't really know how else one would write this story.

I don't know what to say about this. Just have an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mozart sex toys (??), Mozart blow-up dolls (????)

What is life?

I hope this wins. This is the SCP-4000 we deserve.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Despite this, it's still a masterpiece for the ages, and it's proof that you can rush your projects last-minute and have them still be outstanding if you work hard and make good use of your natural gifts!"

thinking emoji

item.title: Re: RESERVED FOR AUTHOR POST
item.content:encoded: Well done. I was hoping you'd get this finished.

item.title: RESERVED FOR AUTHOR POST
item.content:encoded: I'll fill this in later because my hands are dying. This entry will likely be updated later with more content and all that. Pacing issues will also be fixed soon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good job! I knew you could make it on time! This was a really intriguing read and the art killed me so +1. Keep up the hard work!
